title: Predecessor of a Node in BST

content.problemStatement:
Determine the inorder predecessor of a given node `X` in a binary search tree. The inorder predecessor, denoted as node `Y`, is the node that immediately precedes `X` in the inorder traversal of the binary tree. If no such predecessor exists, return `-1`.

For eg., the inorder predecessor of node 6 is node 5 in the following BST:




Input Format

The first line of the input contains two space separated integers NNN and XXX — the number of nodes in the binary search tree, and the node whose predecessor we're looking for.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on a single line - the predecessor node value of node XXX, if it exists, else `-1`.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers N and X — the number of nodes in the BST and the value whose inorder predecessor is to be found. The next N-1 lines each contain two integers pi ci and a character Ri separated by spaces, describing an edge where pi is the parent node, ci is the child node and Ri is 'L' if ci is the left child of pi, otherwise 'R'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the inorder predecessor of X if it exists, otherwise -1.

solution.approach: For each test case we first reconstruct the BST from the given edges. While reading edges we store every node in a HashMap<Integer,Node> and keep a Set of all child values to later identify the root (the only node that never appears as a child). Once the root is known we can find the inorder predecessor of X without an explicit inorder traversal. Starting from the root we move down the tree: if the current node's value is smaller than X, it is a candidate predecessor and we move to its right child; otherwise we move to its left child. The last stored candidate is the greatest value smaller than X, i.e., the inorder predecessor. If no candidate was stored, the answer is -1. This works because in a BST all values in the left subtree are smaller and all values in the right subtree are larger than the node's value.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) to build the tree per test case and O(H) to find the predecessor, where H is the height of the BST (worst case O(N)). Overall O(N) per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the nodes and auxiliary structures per test case.
